name: MIT Cog
short_description: Humanoid upper-torso robot at MIT that pioneered embodied social learning and became the direct predecessor of Kismet and the field of social robotics.
long_description: Cog (short for 'cognition') was an upper-torso humanoid robot developed at MIT's AI Laboratory beginning in 1993 by Rodney Brooks, Lynn Andrea Stein, and a team of graduate students including Cynthia Breazeal. With 21 degrees of freedom and sensory systems spanning vision, auditory, vestibular, kinesthetic, and tactile modalities, Cog was designed to learn through social interaction the way human infants do. It demonstrated face tracking, saccadic eye movements, joint attention, and motor imitation — capabilities that became foundational to the social robotics field. Cog ran until 2003; Cynthia Breazeal's work on the platform directly led to Kismet, the first robot designed explicitly around social-emotional interaction.
ai_note: Vision-based face tracking, saccadic eye control, and motor imitation learning were implemented; no large language models or contemporary ML — classical machine learning and behavior-based control.

images.caption: Cog (1993–2004) on display at the MIT Museum, Cambridge, Massachusetts.
images.alt_text: Humanoid upper-torso robot with a silver and white body, two camera eyes, and articulated arms, displayed on a museum pedestal.

sources.title: Cog (project) — Wikipedia
sources.access_notes: Overview article with references to primary MIT sources.

sources.title: MIT team is building a humanoid robot
sources.access_notes: Details face tracking, imitation, and saccadic eye movements; names key researchers.

sources.title: The Cog Project: Building a Humanoid Robot
sources.access_notes: Primary technical paper by Rodney Brooks and colleagues describing Cog's design and capabilities.

sources.title: Building Brains for Bodies (MIT AI Lab Memo AIM-1439)
sources.access_notes: Original 1993 memo by Brooks and Stein establishing the Cog project rationale.

interactions.title: Catch its eye and it saccades to you
interactions.description: Move into Cog's field of view and its camera eyes snap toward you with rapid saccades, then hold and track the motion — the same orient-and-fixate reflex the team modeled on human infants.

interactions.title: Nod at it and it nods back
interactions.description: Cog could find a face and eyes, register a caregiver's head nod, and imitate it — a first step toward joint attention and shared social reference rather than a scripted gesture.

interactions.title: Hold out a target and watch it reach
interactions.description: Present a visual target and Cog reaches its arm toward it, coordinating hand and eye — the kind of early sensorimotor skill the researchers noted children acquire very young.

interactions.title: Swap its head and it relearns to look
interactions.description: When experimenters fitted Cog with a new head that moved differently, it re-learned its saccade calibration rather than failing — adapting the eye-movement skill to the changed mechanics.

interactions.title: Hand it a Slinky and it plays
interactions.description: Driven by simple coupled neural oscillators rather than any model of the object, Cog could pass a Slinky back and forth between its two hands, tune its arm to the resonant frequency of a swinging pendulum, turn a crank, and beat a drum — settling into the rhythm of whatever it was holding.
interactions.source_label: Williamson, Rhythmic Robot Arm Control Using Oscillators (IROS 1998)

interactions.title: Walk up and talk to it
interactions.description: In the MIT lab, people would approach Cog, speak to it, and look it in the eye; the researchers treated those unscripted encounters as a rich stream of data for the robot to learn social behavior from, the way an infant learns from caregivers.

interactions.title: Turn a hand crank you offer it
interactions.description: Because its rhythmic controller entrains to an object's dynamics without modeling it, you could clip Cog's hand to an unfamiliar crank, wheel, or mechanism and have it find and sustain the right turning rhythm on its own — a hands-on way to probe how far model-free entrainment generalizes.

interactions.title: A shared-gaze pointing game
interactions.description: Building on its demonstrated face-finding and head-nod imitation, a person could glance toward an object and prompt Cog to follow the gaze and look there too, turning joint attention into a simple back-and-forth pointing game with a caregiver.
